S01E01.title: S01E01
S01E01.content:
Introducing Touching Water

We started this podcast because we are interested in exploring how to approach water.
We are Desirée and Márk, two water researchers. Márk, as a trained data scientist, is fascinated by water’s dynamic presence—from subsurface flows to atmospheric moisture, exploring interfaces where rain becomes groundwater. Desirée, as a trained anthropologist, tries to understand hydrosocial relations, how different people work with water, and what happens when we see water as more than a resource.
We talk to each other about how we can approach water and with our guests explore how different scientific and everyday experts work with water—through measuring, modeling, fieldwork, or laboratory analysis. We learn about current challenges and research interests, understanding why it’s important to think with water across disciplines and with the wider public.
More info on Desirée Hetzel: www.sts.sot.tum.de/en/sts/people/researchers/desiree-hetzel/
More info on Márk Somogyvári: www.iri-thesys.org/people-pages/mark-somogyvari/
Info on our producer Pauline Münch: www.iri-thesys.org/people-pages/pauline-muench/

S01E02.title: S01E02
S01E02.content:
Urban Flooding and Digital Models: A Conversation with Franzi

From civil engineering student to urban flood researcher, Franzi Tügel demonstrates how water moves through cities in unexpected ways. Her fascination with water-related topics during her studies led her to focus on fluvial flooding—when heavy rainfall overwhelms urban systems rather than rivers bursting their banks.
Franzi sees water through digital simulation, using two-dimensional hydrodynamic models to track how water flows and accumulates across Berlin’s sealed surfaces. Her approach to touching water is fundamentally computational—she encounters water through virtual representations that predict flooding patterns and help cities prepare for increasingly intense rainfall events.
Following devastating events like Berlin’s 2016 floods and the 2021 Ahrtal catastrophe, Franzi’s modeling work becomes crucial for thinking with flooding in terms of adaptation. Her approach involves creating digital representations of real landscapes, then situating these models within specific urban contexts. She touches water and flooding through bringing simulations into concrete flood management.
For more info on Franziska Tügel:
Franzi brought us a song about water:

S01E03.title: S01E03
S01E03.content:
Flash Droughts and Community Voices: A Conversation with Pedro

From drought-prone Brazil to water-rich Germany, eco-hydrologist Pedro Alencar studies flash droughts through an unusual dual approach. While analyzing satellite data and cross-correlating databases to track rapid soil moisture loss, he simultaneously engages directly with local farmers and communities—a practice uncommon among natural scientists.
Pedro’s research in Brandenburg reveals how flash droughts challenge traditional water-abundant regions. But rather than relying solely on modeling, he incorporates farmers’ lived experiences and perceptions into his scientific framework. This community-engaged methodology enriches his data analysis while helping tailor practical solutions like water harvesting and nature-based mitigation strategies.
His approach demonstrates how touching water means understanding both its measurable patterns and its dialogue with local experts.
For more info on Pedro Alencar: www.tu.berlin/en/ai-climate-land-use/about/team/dr-pedro-alencar

S01E04.title: S01E04
S01E04.content:
Water Governance in Multiple Voices: A Conversation with Kei

Water governance researcher Kei Namba brings a unique cross-cultural perspective, contrasting Japan’s disaster-driven risk awareness with Germany’s approach to water management. Her work in the Berlin-Brandenburg region reveals water’s “multiplicity”—how the same resource serves urban supply, irrigation, energy, mining, and tourism, each with different stakeholder interests.
Through qualitative content analysis of policy documents and media reports, Kei examines how diverse voices can align around common water goals. She critiques purely technocratic solutions, advocating instead for governance approaches that include local participation. Her research touches water through institutional analysis, studying actors and power dynamics rather than physical systems.
From Berlin’s “sponge city” initiative to the citizen-driven Berlin Water Charter, Kei demonstrates how civil society can demand transparency and accountability in water services.
Kei Namba brought us activated charcoal as her personal water item—a simple filtering tool that embodies her belief in accessible, sustainable water practices that bridge scientific research and everyday environmental action.

index.title: Home
index.content:
Touching Water: a Science Podcast
Welcome to the Touching Water Podcast portal! Here you can find all our episodes, shownotes and extra information about our podcast.

In ‘touching water’ an anthropologist and data scientist explore water with experts who work intimately with a well-known element. From hydrologists and engineers to local water experts and urban planners, our guests reveal how different people can approach, understand, and communicate about water.
What emerges is not just knowledge about H2O, but new ways of seeing our relationship with water and people. Because how we touch water shapes how we understand our world.
Hosts: Desirée Hetzel and Márk Somogyvári
Supported by the recording studio at CMS at HU Berlin and the Geo.X network

Touching Water Lite
In this spinoff, Desirée and Márk discusses interesting articles, papers and books from an interdisciplinary perspective. With their dialog they try to find a common understanding of current topics on water science.
